one_line_takeaway: Federating a state-of-the-art LSTM-autoencoder CAN IDS (CANdito) via FedAvg/MQTT-over-5G achieves detection performance close to (but consistently below) the centralized version, at the cost of measurably higher per-vehicle communication overhead that scales with the number of rounds needed to converge.
key_finding: Increasing the number of vehicles V delays convergence (in terms of communication rounds needed) while increasing local epochs E marginally accelerates it, matching the expectation that higher E / lower V more closely approximates centralized learning; FedProx's proximal term did not improve performance over FedAvg and higher mu values slowed convergence further, so FedAvg was used for the main comparison. On the most decentralized configuration tested (V=50, E=1), federated CANdito showed detection capability close to but below the centralized version, with a marginally greater spread of FPR (around 0.1) but a lower median FPR than the centralized model across CAN IDs. Model updates were highly homogeneous in size: mean 372,893 bytes (std 8 bytes; min 372,862, max 372,898 bytes). Measured MQTT latency (Table II): subscriber (global update receipt) averaged 0.180s (std 0.040s, median 0.169s, min 0.066s, max 0.726s); publisher (local update send) averaged 0.411s (std 0.149s, median 0.365s, min 0.229s, max 2.990s). Per-vehicle communication overhead varied substantially by CAN ID depending on the number of rounds R needed to converge under early stopping (Table III), e.g. ID 0FF converged in R=23 rounds with download 8.53 MiB / upload 8.18 MiB (delta +14.66 MiB vs. centralized), while ID 0FE required R=183 rounds with download 65.43 MiB / upload 65.08 MiB (delta +128.45 MiB) -- in every one of the 13 tested IDs the federated setting required strictly more raw data exchange per vehicle than the equivalent centralized upload-then-download scenario.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is a federated-learning (FL) version of CANdito, a pre-existing state-of-the-art LSTM-autoencoder-based intrusion detection system (IDS) for Controller Area Network (CAN) traffic (CANdito is cited as prior work by the same research group, ref [1], not introduced here). The authors build a full FL pipeline around CANdito: an MQTT-5.0-over-TLS publish/subscribe communication infrastructure (Eclipse Mosquitto broker) connecting vehicles to a Global Server over 5G, a federated aggregation strategy using FedAvg or FedProx, a decentralized threshold-computation scheme for the reconstruction-error anomaly threshold, and a federated early-stopping mechanism based on averaged local validation MSE loss. They then empirically compare the federated CANdito against a centralized (CL) version of the identical algorithm trained on the same dataset, evaluating (1) convergence behavior of FedAvg vs. FedProx across varying numbers of participating vehicles (V=5,10,20,50) and local epochs (E=1,3,5), (2) detection rate (DR) and false-positive rate (FPR) of federated vs. centralized CANdito, and (3) the communication overhead (latency and data volume) of the FL training process over a real 5G network with the broker hosted remotely in London while vehicles/tests were run in Milan.
threat_model: The paper addresses the standard CAN bus threat model established by prior CAN-IDS literature (not newly defined here): CAN lacks native authentication or cryptographic protection, so an attacker with bus access can perform (1) Denial-of-Service attacks flooding the bus with high-priority messages, (2) injection attacks inserting unauthorized messages (including replay attacks and 'seamless change' attacks that gradually shift signal values), (3) drop attacks deleting legitimate packets, and (4) masquerade attacks combining drop+injection to impersonate a legitimate ECU while preserving arrival timing. Attacks for evaluation were synthetically injected into the validation/test splits using the pre-existing CANtack tool (also from prior work, refs [1],[16]): sequences of 25 injected packets (fuzzing, replay, signal-extreme-shift) and drop attacks removing 25 consecutive packets of a targeted ID. A secondary, non-adversarial 'privacy threat' also motivates the FL design: sending raw in-vehicle CAN data to a centralized training server exposes potentially sensitive vehicle/driver information, which FL mitigates by keeping raw data local and sharing only model weight updates (secured via MQTT over TLS with authentication).
limitations: The paper's own discussion and results note: (1) federated CANdito's detection performance is consistently slightly lower than the centralized version, attributed to approximations introduced by federated training; (2) the FL process incurs a significant net communication overhead in raw data volume per vehicle compared to centralized learning, because of the number of rounds required for convergence and the relatively small local dataset per vehicle in highly decentralized settings (V=50); (3) per-vehicle threshold aggregation across nodes was found to substantially degrade performance and was abandoned in favor of single-vehicle threshold computation, indicating sensitivity of CANdito's reconstruction-error threshold to decentralized estimation; (4) some validation results referenced (FedProx convergence speed comparison, early-stopping hyperparameter sensitivity) are explicitly stated as 'omitted due to space constraints' and not shown quantitatively in the paper; (5) evaluation used a synthetic, tool-injected attack set (CANtack) rather than attacks observed in the wild; (6) the dataset comes from a single vehicle model/dataset (ReCAN, one Alfa Romeo Giulia Veloce) recorded across 9 sessions from one vehicle, so cross-vehicle heterogeneity (a core FL concern) is simulated by partitioning one vehicle's driving-session data across virtual clients rather than using data from genuinely distinct vehicles.

datasets_used: ReCAN C-1 (real-world CAN traffic recorded in an Alfa Romeo Giulia Veloce; 9 driving sessions; 25,082,275 total packets; sessions 1,2,6,8,9 = train, session 5 = validation, session 7 = test; 13 CAN IDs evaluated)
quantitative_results: Average FL model update size: 372,893 bytes (std 8 bytes; range 372,862-372,898 bytes)
MQTT subscriber (global update) latency: mean 0.180s, std 0.040s, median 0.169s, min 0.066s, max 0.726s
MQTT publisher (local update) latency: mean 0.411s, std 0.149s, median 0.365s, min 0.229s, max 2.990s

Per-vehicle communication overhead ranged from R=23 rounds / 8.53 MiB DL / 8.18 MiB UL / +14.66 MiB delta (CAN ID 0FF) to R=183 rounds / 65.43 MiB DL / 65.08 MiB UL / +128.45 MiB delta (CAN ID 0FE), computed at V=50, E=1

Federated CANdito (V=50, E=1, FedAvg) achieved detection rate close to but below centralized CANdito across the 13 tested CAN IDs, with a marginally wider FPR spread around 0.1 but a lower median FPR than the centralized model

FedAvg trained for 200 communication rounds; FedProx trained for 500 rounds due to slower observed convergence; increasing the FedProx proximal term mu (tested at 1, 0.1, 0.01, 0.001) further slowed convergence without improving performance
baselines_compared: Centralized (CL) version of the same CANdito LSTM-autoencoder IDS, trained on the same ReCAN C-1 dataset, used as the primary detection-performance and communication-overhead baseline
FedAvg vs. FedProx compared as alternative federated aggregation strategies (both pre-existing algorithms, not proposed by this paper)
